content.problemStatement:
You are given a tree with nnn vertices numbered 1,2,…,n1, 2, \ldots, n1,2,…,n. There are two players Alice and Bob who play a game on this tree, taking turns alternatively. Alice starts.

In their turn, a participant has to remove a leaf (and the edge adjacent to it) from the tree. A leaf is a node with degree 111. Please note that the tree changes with the game progressing, and a node which is not a leaf in the beginning can become a leaf later and become eligible to be removed.

Alice likes the vertex xxx and wants it to be the last node remaining. Bob hates node xxx and doesn't want it to be the last node remaining.

For all the choices of vertex xxx, find who wins assuming both play optimally.


Input Format
There are multiple independent testcases.



The first line contains TTT, the number of testcases.
The first line of each testcase contains a single integer nnn, the number of vertices.
Each of the next n−1n - 1n−1 lines contains two integers denoting the endpoints of an edge. Each edge is present only once.


Output Format
For each testcase, print a string of length nnn on a new line. The ithi^{\text{th}}ith character in this string should be 111 if Alice wins the game for x=ix=ix=i, and 000 if Bob wins.
constraints: The sum of nnn over all testcases doesn't exceed 10510^5105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
In the first testcase:

If x=1x = 1x=1, Alice removes node 222 and wins.
If x=2x = 2x=2, Alice removes node 111 and wins.

In the second testcase:

Consider x=1x=1x=1. If Alice removes node 222, Bob can remove node 111 in his turn and win. If Alice removes node 444, Bob then removes node 222. Then in her turn, Alice can only remove node 555 leaving two nodes 111 and 333. Bob can now remove node 111 and win. The game proceeds similarly if Alice removes node 555 first. So, Bob wins the game.
One can see that for other choices of xxx as well, Bob wins.
